true_relatives.note: Hindi कितने descends from Sanskrit kati (how many) — the same word used to ask 'how many bells have struck'

useful_coincidences.note: Russian uses 'which hour' to ask the time; Hindi uses 'how many o'clock' — both languages frame time-asking through the clock unit rather than an abstract 'time' word

sound_alikes.note: 'kitne' starts like 'kitten' — imagine a kitten sitting on a clock asking 'how many?'

root_breakdown.logic: The phrase asks 'how many bell-strikes?' — since बजे comes from बजना (to ring), कितने बजे literally asks 'how many have rung?', i.e., what time is it.

etymology_story: कितने comes from Sanskrit kati, meaning 'how many', preserved across two thousand years of language change.
बजे comes from बजना (to ring), reflecting the tradition of striking a bell for each hour.
Together, कितने बजे means 'how many have rung?' — a beautifully concrete way to ask the time.
The phrase is the standard conversational question for asking what time something happens or is happening now.

pronunciation_guide.stress: light stress on 'kit'; the phrase flows as two words

tips.tip: 'kit' at the start of कितने sounds like the English word 'kit'
'baje' at the end rhymes with 'beige' — the 'j' has a soft 'zh' quality

memory_hook.primary: A cartoon clock face with a giant question mark where the hands should be, surrounded by small ringing bells — each bell has a number on it (1, 2, 3...), and they are all mid-ring.
memory_hook.connection: The question mark on the clock face represents कितने (how many), and the ringing bells represent बजे (o'clock, from 'to ring') — together encoding 'how many rings = what time?'

examples.hindi: तुम्हारी ट्रेन कितने बजे है?
examples.romanisation: tumhārī ṭren kitne baje hai?
examples.english: What time is your train?
examples.literal: your train how-many o'clock is

examples.hindi: मीटिंग कितने बजे शुरू होती है?
examples.romanisation: miṭiṃg kitne baje śurū hotī hai?
examples.english: What time does the meeting start?
examples.literal: meeting how-many o'clock start happens is

examples.hindi: तुम कल कितने बजे आओगे?
examples.romanisation: tum kal kitne baje āoge?
examples.english: What time will you come tomorrow?
examples.literal: you tomorrow how-many o'clock will-come

examples.hindi: दुकान कितने बजे बंद होती है?
examples.romanisation: dukān kitne baje band hotī hai?
examples.english: What time does the shop close?
examples.literal: shop how-many o'clock closed happens is

true_relatives.note: Hindi आधा comes directly from Sanskrit ardha (half) — one of the most ancient and stable words in the language
Persian uses نیم (nīm) for half, which appears in Hindi compounds like नीम-रात (midnight, literally half-night) — a parallel concept

useful_coincidences.note: No shared ancestry, but both Hebrew khatsi and Hindi ādhā are single short words for 'half' — the simplicity helps anchor the concept

sound_alikes.note: In South Indian languages and in Sanskrit, ardha means half — near-identical in meaning, and audibly close to ādhā

root_breakdown.logic: The Sanskrit 'r' in ardha softened and disappeared through Prakrit and Apabhraṁśa, leaving the smooth ādhā. The meaning remained perfectly stable.

etymology_story: The word goes back to Sanskrit ardha, one of the basic mathematical vocabulary words of ancient India.
Through Prakrit it became addha, then smoothed further into ādha in later medieval speech.
Modern Hindi आधा retains the same meaning without any drift — half is half.
In time expressions, आधा appears in compounds: आधा घंटा (half an hour), डेढ़ (one and a half), and ढाई (two and a half).

pronunciation_guide.stress: both syllables are long vowels; stress is equal and gentle

tips.tip: if you know the Sanskrit ardha (half), ādhā is simply ardha with the 'r' softened away

memory_hook.primary: A pizza sliced perfectly in two halves — one half sitting on a plate, the other half still in the air, frozen mid-fall. The cut is razor-straight.
memory_hook.connection: The clean split of the pizza into two equal halves is the most direct image of आधा — the Sanskrit root ardha meant exactly 'one of two equal parts'.

examples.hindi: मुझे आधा घंटा और चाहिए।
examples.romanisation: mujhe ādhā ghaṇṭā aur cāhie.
examples.english: I need another half an hour.
examples.literal: me-to half hour more need

examples.hindi: आधी रोटी मेरे लिए काफ़ी है।
examples.romanisation: ādhī roṭī mere lie kāfī hai.
examples.english: Half a flatbread is enough for me.
examples.literal: half flatbread for-me enough is

examples.hindi: वह आधे रास्ते में रुक गया।
examples.romanisation: vah ādhe rāste mẽ ruk gayā.
examples.english: He stopped halfway.
examples.literal: he half road in stopped went

examples.hindi: साढ़े तीन बजे मिलते हैं।
examples.romanisation: sāṛhe tīn baje milte haĩ.
examples.english: Let's meet at half past three.
examples.literal: half-past three o'clock meet are

true_relatives.meaning: minus a quarter-foot / minus one fourth
true_relatives.note: पौने comes from Sanskrit pādona: pāda (foot / quarter) + ūna (minus, lacking). So पौने means 'one quarter lacking' = quarter to.

useful_coincidences.note: Sounds similar to 'paune' — imagine a pawn piece pointing to the 'quarter to' position on a clock

sound_alikes.note: 'paune' sounds like 'pawn' with an extra vowel — a chess pawn standing at the quarter-to position on a clock

root.meaning: minus one quarter (pāda = quarter; ūna = minus/lacking)

root_breakdown.logic: pāda meant 'foot' but was also the Sanskrit word for one-quarter. ūna means 'lacking/minus'. So pādona = minus a quarter = three-quarters = quarter to the next hour.

etymology_story: The word comes from Sanskrit pādona: pāda (a quarter) + ūna (lacking, minus).
It originally described any quantity that was one-quarter less than a whole — 'lacking a fourth'.
In time-telling, it came to mean the moment when a quarter-hour is still lacking before the next hour.
Today पौने always precedes the next hour: पौने चार = quarter to four (i.e., 3:45).

pronunciation_guide.stress: stress on the first syllable 'pau'; the 'au' is a rounded 'ow' sound

tips.tip: the first syllable 'pau' sounds like the English word 'pawn' — same rounded vowel

memory_hook.primary: A clock showing 3:45, with a chess pawn piece placed on the clock face right at the 45-minute mark, pointing upward toward the 12.
memory_hook.connection: The pawn (paune sounds like 'pawn') sits at the quarter-to position — 15 minutes before the hour — visually encoding both the sound and the meaning of पौने.

examples.hindi: वह पौने नौ बजे आया।
examples.romanisation: vah paune nau baje āyā.
examples.english: He came at quarter to nine.
examples.literal: he quarter-to nine o'clock came

examples.hindi: ट्रेन पौने आठ बजे रवाना होगी।
examples.romanisation: ṭren paune āṭh baje ravānā hogī.
examples.english: The train will depart at quarter to eight.
examples.literal: train quarter-to eight o'clock depart will-be

examples.hindi: पौने तीन बजे तक काम ख़त्म करो।
examples.romanisation: paune tīn baje tak kām khatm karo.
examples.english: Finish the work by quarter to three.
examples.literal: quarter-to three o'clock until work finish do

examples.hindi: मेरी घड़ी में पौने बारह बज रहे हैं।
examples.romanisation: merī ghaṛī mẽ paune bārah baj rahe haĩ.
examples.english: My watch shows quarter to twelve.
examples.literal: my watch in quarter-to twelve ringing are

true_relatives.meaning: one and a quarter (sa = with; pāda = quarter)
true_relatives.note: सवा comes from Sanskrit sapāda, meaning 'with a quarter added' — one whole plus one quarter

useful_coincidences.note: No shared meaning, but the sound resemblance to 'savā' can help anchor the word phonetically

sound_alikes.note: 'savā' sounds like the first part of 'savvy' — a 'savvy' person always knows exactly what time it is, right down to the quarter-hour

root.meaning: with a quarter added (sa = with; pāda = quarter/foot)

root_breakdown.logic: Where पौने means 'one quarter less', सवा means 'one quarter more' — the mirror image. It always refers to the hour just stated plus fifteen minutes.

etymology_story: सवा comes from Sanskrit sapāda, a mathematical term meaning 'one and a quarter' (sa = with, pāda = quarter).
Prakrit simplified it to savā, preserving the meaning of 'something plus a quarter portion'.
In time-telling, 'quarter past' is exactly one hour plus one quarter-hour — so सवा became the standard term.
It is the mirror image of पौने (quarter to): पौने takes away a quarter, सवा adds one.

pronunciation_guide.stress: stress on the second syllable 'vā' (long vowel); first syllable is light

tips.tip: the first syllable 'sa' is the same as the 'sa' in English 'savvy'

memory_hook.primary: A clock showing 1:15, with a golden quarter-circle wedge added to the right side of the clock face, like an extra slice of pie being slipped in after the main hour.
memory_hook.connection: The wedge added to the clock represents the Sanskrit sapāda meaning 'with a quarter added' — visually showing how सवा = the hour plus a quarter.

examples.hindi: वह सवा दस बजे आया।
examples.romanisation: vah savā das baje āyā.
examples.english: He arrived at quarter past ten.
examples.literal: he quarter-past ten o'clock arrived

examples.hindi: बस सवा तीन बजे मिलेगी।
examples.romanisation: bas savā tīn baje milegī.
examples.english: The bus will come at quarter past three.
examples.literal: bus quarter-past three o'clock will-meet

examples.hindi: सवा एक बजे खाना खाते हैं।
examples.romanisation: savā ek baje khānā khāte haĩ.
examples.english: We eat lunch at quarter past one.
examples.literal: quarter-past one o'clock food eat are

examples.hindi: दफ़्तर सवा नौ बजे खुलता है।
examples.romanisation: daftar savā nau baje khultā hai.
examples.english: The office opens at quarter past nine.
examples.literal: office quarter-past nine o'clock opens is

true_relatives.meaning: one and a half (literally: half of the second = 1.5)
true_relatives.note: Hindi डेढ़ is a heavily contracted descendant of this Sanskrit compound meaning 'one plus half of the next'

useful_coincidences.meaning: half past one (literally: half of the second hour)
useful_coincidences.note: Russian expresses half past one as 'half of the second', using the same logic as the Sanskrit source of डेढ़ — both count the half-unit of the upcoming hour

sound_alikes.note: ḍeṛh sounds a bit like 'dare' — it takes daring to remember that this unique word means only 1.5, nothing else

root.meaning: half of the second (= one and a half)

root_breakdown.logic: The Sanskrit form used a fractional counting system: 'half of the second unit' = one full + half of one = 1.5. The massive contraction over centuries produced the short, unique word डेढ़.

etymology_story: The Sanskrit source was ardhadvitiīya, a mathematical phrase meaning 'half of the second' — i.e., one whole plus half the next unit.
This fractional counting system (similar to how Russian says 'half of the second hour' for 1:30) was once common across Indian languages.
Through Prakrit and Apabhraṁśa, the long compound contracted dramatically, shedding syllables over centuries.
The result is the unique Hindi word डेढ़ — used only for 1.5, never for any other quantity.

pronunciation_guide.stress: single syllable; the ḍ is retroflex (tongue curled back); the ṛh is a breathy retroflex flap

tips.tip: start like English 'dare' but retroflex the 'd' (curl tongue back) and end with a breathy 'rh' flap

memory_hook.primary: A clock showing exactly 1:30, with the minute hand pointing straight down to the 6. The hour hand stands at 1, and a thick red line bisects the space between 1 and 2 — showing the halfway point.
memory_hook.connection: The bisected space between 1 and 2 on the clock face mirrors the Sanskrit meaning of डेढ़: 'half of the second' = one and a half, fixed permanently at 1:30.

examples.hindi: वह डेढ़ बजे आएगा।
examples.romanisation: vah ḍeṛh baje āegā.
examples.english: He will come at half past one.
examples.literal: he one-and-a-half o'clock will-come

examples.hindi: डेढ़ बजे खाना तैयार होगा।
examples.romanisation: ḍeṛh baje khānā taiyār hogā.
examples.english: The food will be ready at half past one.
examples.literal: one-thirty o'clock food ready will-be

examples.hindi: दोपहर के बाद डेढ़ बजे से मीटिंग है।
examples.romanisation: dopahar ke bād ḍeṛh baje se miṭiṃg hai.
examples.english: The meeting is from half past one in the afternoon.
examples.literal: noon after one-thirty o'clock from meeting is

examples.hindi: डेढ़ घंटे में काम ख़त्म हो जाएगा।
examples.romanisation: ḍeṛh ghaṇṭe mẽ kām khatm ho jāegā.
examples.english: The work will be done in an hour and a half.
examples.literal: one-and-a-half hour in work finish become will-go
